_meta.description:
Content for tools/reference.html. Each entry under `cards` is keyed by a card-id (matching data-card-id in the HTML) and rendered into the side drawer when that card is clicked. The body_html field accepts inline HTML — paragraphs (

), headings (

), lists (
    /
      ), , , ,
      . Edit this file and commit; the deck reads it at runtime.

heading: Reference
lede: The operating system underneath the deck. Five pillars — three miles, the modular industry argument, and the 10-year expansion plan. Click any card to open its readme; pills cross-reference the slide(s) where the topic is presented.

energy-efficiency.title: Energy Efficiency by Default
energy-efficiency.subtitle: Net-zero-ready as the floor, not the upgrade
energy-efficiency.body_html:
Every FBX home is engineered to outperform local energy code by 20–30% out of the box. Panelization tightens the thermal envelope; the kit includes high-R wall and roof assemblies, low-U glazing, and pre-routed pathways for heat-pump HVAC and balanced ventilation.

Why it matters to the buyer

  • Lower utility bills from day one — typical owner saves $1,400–$2,200/yr
  • Federal and state tax incentives stack on the purchase
  • Documented 5–7% resale premium for code-plus performance

Why it matters to the franchise

  • One spec everywhere: no per-region engineering rework
  • Energy compliance is a paperwork problem we solve once

ease-of-design.title: Ease of Design
ease-of-design.subtitle: Guided, not gated
ease-of-design.body_html:
The customer configures the home from a kit-of-parts AI-assisted menu — floorplans, finish trims, glazing packages — instead of starting from a blank idea. The constraint set is bounded by what the factory can build, so every choice is automatically buildable, permittable, and quoted.

What changes for the customer

  • From weeks of architect back-and-forth to hours of guided configuration
  • Real-time price + timeline updates as choices change
  • Confidence that the result is real, not a render

speed.title: Speed
speed.subtitle: Fast permits, AI-assisted compliance
speed.body_html:
We work upstream with permitting offices to land master-permits where possible, and use AI-assisted compliance checks to surface red flags before submission rather than after.

Permit-to-pour: target 30–45 days

Versus the 90–180 days typical for conventional custom builds in the same markets. Speed compounds at the franchise level — faster permits mean faster revenue recognition mean faster reinvestment.

customization.title: Customization Within Constraints
customization.subtitle: Trust the brand · love the iteration
customization.body_html:
The cars-and-trims analogy: customers want the trust of a known brand AND the uniqueness of personalizing their home. Our kit-of-parts is the platform; the configurator is the trim sheet.Just like cars & trucks the frame underneath is the same, but the lipstick is different every time a new model is built.

What's customizable

  • Floorplan (within a curated set per product line)
  • Roof profile, cladding, fenestration package
  • Interior finishes, fixtures, appliance tier
  • Site siting, solar/storage integration

What's not

  • Structural envelope (panel sizes, span tables)
  • Mechanical pathways (the spine that keeps the kit shippable)

rd.title: Research & Development
rd.body_html:
R&D pipeline is product-led: we ship what the franchise can sell and the factory can produce. Investment areas:

  • Next-generation panel chemistries (eSIPs, hybrid CFS)
  • Pod modularization (bath/kitchen wet-modules)
  • Configurator UX and rules engine
  • Energy systems integration

R&D output is gated by demonstrated demand from franchise partners — we don't build features without an installer asking for them.

engineers.title: Engineers
engineers.subtitle: Centralized engineering, distributed execution
engineers.body_html:
Structural, mechanical, and energy engineering happen once, at FBX Core. Franchisees don't need PE staff on payroll — they get the stamped drawings and compliance docs as part of the kit.

Implications

  • One engineering org for the whole network — leverage compounds
  • Franchisees scale faster (no PE bottleneck)
  • Standards travel with the kit, not with the franchisee

regulatory-approvals.title: Regulatory Approvals
regulatory-approvals.subtitle: We do this once, not 137 times
regulatory-approvals.body_html:
Each Home-Fab opens with a regional approvals package already in hand: code path, factory inspection regime, cross-jurisdictional acceptance.

We work with state housing offices early to land:

  • Modular factory certification (per-state, recurring)
  • Master plan approvals for the standard product line
  • Acceptance of FBX QA in lieu of in-field re-inspection
The cost of compliance, amortized across the network, is what creates the durable cost advantage.

home-sales.title: Home Sales
home-sales.body_html:
The franchise owns the customer relationship; FBX Core powers the supply side. Sales channel mix:

  • Direct-to-consumer — configurator, showroom, retained customer
  • Builder partnerships — small/mid builders use us as their factory
  • Developer projects — multi-unit and infill

franchise-sales.title: Franchise Sales
franchise-sales.subtitle: Selling the playbook, not just the kit
franchise-sales.body_html:
Franchise candidates buy a complete operating package: site selection, regional permit approvals, training, supply contracts, marketing, and ongoing technical support.

Target franchisee profile

  • Existing GC, builder, or developer with regional reputation
  • Capable of $4–8M in working capital plus site investment
  • Wants to scale beyond what custom-build can produce

training.title: Training
training.subtitle: Kit assembly is the curriculum
training.body_html:
The construction workforce needs to know kit assembly, not bespoke build. That's a much shorter learning curve.

Two-track program

  • Crew certification — 4-week program, panel handling, sequence, QC
  • Site supervisor — 8-week program, multi-trade coordination, inspections

Training is run regionally out of each Home-Fab. Graduates feed the franchise network's labor pool.

factory-standard.title: Factory Standard Design
factory-standard.subtitle: One spec across the network
factory-standard.body_html:
The standard design is the contract between Core, the suppliers, and the franchise. It defines:

  • Panel sizes and connection details
  • Pod interfaces (plumbing, electrical, HVAC stub-outs)
  • Tolerance bands for site-built work
  • QA acceptance criteria

When the standard updates, every Home-Fab updates. Versioning is centralized.

sips.title: SIPs — Structural Insulated Panels
sips.subtitle: Primary panelization technology
sips.body_html:
Our default wall and roof system. SIPs deliver superior thermal performance, faster on-site assembly, and dimensional consistency that conventional framing can't match.

Suppliers

  • Multiple domestic mills under abstraction-layer contracts
  • Ramping foreign-sourced supply for Tier-2 cost competition

Why SIPs first

  • Mature supply chain, known performance
  • Compatible with conventional finish materials
  • Permits and codes already accommodate them

cold-formed-steel.title: Cold-Formed Steel
cold-formed-steel.subtitle: Panelization · option track
cold-formed-steel.body_html:
CFS panel system as an alternative for fire-rated and seismic-priority markets. Selectively deployed where SIPs aren't optimal.

  • Fire performance (Type II/III construction)
  • Seismic and high-wind regions
  • Multi-family applications above 4 stories

esips.title: eSIPs — Enhanced SIPs
esips.subtitle: Next-gen panel chemistry
esips.body_html:
Higher R-values, better moisture management, and embedded service raceways. In R&D today; staged rollout planned for Year 3+.

Strategic value: as energy codes tighten further, eSIPs become standard. Owning the spec lets us ride the regulatory curve instead of getting squeezed by it.

bath-kitchen-pods.title: Bath / Kitchen Pods
bath-kitchen-pods.subtitle: Pre-built wet modules
bath-kitchen-pods.body_html:
The plumbing-heavy parts of the home are built in a controlled factory environment, tested under pressure, and dropped into the panel envelope on site.

Why pods

  • Wet-trade work is the highest-defect zone in conventional construction
  • Factory pressure-test catches what site inspection misses
  • Compresses schedule by ~3 weeks per home

demand-signals.title: Demand Signals
demand-signals.subtitle: What suppliers want from us
demand-signals.body_html:
Suppliers don't want lots of small orders — they want predictable, high-volume, contracted demand. Our aggregation across 137 fabs gives them exactly that, which translates to:

  • Pricing tiers we couldn't access individually
  • Priority allocation during capacity constraints
  • Co-investment in tooling and process improvements

receiving.title: Receiving
receiving.subtitle: Domestic & foreign supply consolidation
receiving.body_html:
The Core warehouse is the consolidation point. Inbound mix:

  • Containers — foreign-sourced panels and pods, port-to-DC
  • Truckload — domestic suppliers, direct-to-DC
  • Dropship-eligible — long-lead items routed straight to Home-Fabs

Receiving runs QA on every inbound: dimensional checks, moisture, finish, manifest match. Failed lots are rejected at the dock, not at the field.

sorting.title: Sorting — Create the Kit of Parts
sorting.body_html:
This is where panels and pods become a kit. Each home order is staged on its own pallet sequence: walls, roof, pods, finish components, fasteners, drawings.

The kit ships out as a ready-to-assemble package, in the order it'll be installed. No on-site sorting, no missing parts, no return trips.

ship-to-home-fab.title: Ship to Home-Fab
ship-to-home-fab.subtitle: Containerized distribution
ship-to-home-fab.body_html:
Each Home-Fab gets a regular cadence of inbound containers — anticipating 2–4 per week at steady state. The container itself becomes a temporary on-site staging unit before being returned to circulation.

~150-mile delivery radius from each Home-Fab covers the lot-side last mile. The Core-to-Fab leg is the long-haul; the Fab-to-Lot leg is local.

franchisee-establishment.title: Franchisee Establishment
franchisee-establishment.subtitle: Home-Fab launch playbook
franchisee-establishment.body_html:
A new franchisee follows a standardized rollout sequence:

  1. Site selection — Core helps identify the regional Fab location
  2. Permitting & build-out — modular Fab structure, ~6–9 months
  3. Hiring & training — crew certification before first delivery
  4. Pilot run — 2–3 homes under Core supervision
  5. Full operations — ramped to design throughput

quality-assurance.title: Quality Assurance
quality-assurance.subtitle: Cross-Fab consistency
quality-assurance.body_html:
QA happens at three points: at the supplier (specification compliance), at Core receiving (manifest and dimensional), and at the Home-Fab (assembly fit-up).

Each home gets a digital QA dossier with photos, measurements, and signoffs. The dossier is the warranty audit trail and the data feed for continuous improvement.

regional-expansion.title: Regional Expansion
regional-expansion.subtitle: Hub-and-spoke rollout
regional-expansion.body_html:
Year 1–3: West Coast hub — California, Pacific NW, Mountain West.
Year 3–6: Midwest and South-Central hubs.
Year 6–10: East Coast and Southeast saturation.

Each hub has a Core DC and 8–15 Home-Fabs in its 500-mile service radius. The 137-fab steady state is reached around Year 8.

See the live 10-Year Expansion Map for the year-by-year plan.

site-work.title: Site Work
site-work.subtitle: Foundation, utilities, prep
site-work.body_html:
The franchise's site team handles everything below the panel: excavation, foundation, utility service, drainage. The factory delivery doesn't happen until site is permit-to-pour ready.

This is the slowest part of the on-site sequence — and the part where local knowledge wins. Franchisees who already do site work in their region just keep doing it; the kit is what changes.

last-mile-logistics.title: Home-Fab to Lot Logistics
last-mile-logistics.subtitle: Last 150 miles
last-mile-logistics.body_html:
From Home-Fab to lot: typically same-day delivery, often direct from Fab loading dock to crane on site. Crane and transport are pre-cleared as part of the franchise's standing logistics agreements.

No double-handling: the kit arrives in the order it'll be installed.

inspections.title: Inspections
inspections.subtitle: Jurisdictional sign-off
inspections.body_html:
Local jurisdiction inspections still happen — but the FBX QA dossier dramatically reduces the back-and-forth. Most jurisdictions accept factory QA in lieu of partial in-field inspection once the master plan is approved.

Critical sign-offs: foundation, rough mechanical, final. Pre-scheduled in the construction calendar.

construction-workforce.title: Construction Workforce
construction-workforce.subtitle: Skilled people · empowered by systems
construction-workforce.body_html:
Behind every structure is a diverse team — engineers, laborers, surveyors, project managers. They want to scale. They demand support. We empower their rise.

Six core roles

  • Engineers — centralized at Core, distributed via stamped drawings
  • Skilled Trades — kit-assembly certified, network-portable
  • Surveyors — site readiness verification
  • Project Managers — multi-trade coordination at the Home-Fab
  • Logistics Operators — Fab-to-lot transport & crane scheduling
  • Permit Specialists — jurisdictional compliance

The training thesis

The construction labor problem isn't volume — it's training simplicity. Conventional builds need every trade to be a craftsman. Kit assembly needs them to follow a sequence and use a torque wrench.

Workforce funnel

  • Recruit from existing trades + new entrants
  • 4-week certification program at the Home-Fab
  • Continuous-learning track for advancement
  • Franchisee-employed, network-portable certification

Network at scale

  • 150-mile franchise service radius
  • 137 fabs at Year 10 (national)
  • 50 states covered (Year 10)
  • 3 BUs · One delivered home

what-went-wrong.title: What Went Wrong
what-went-wrong.subtitle: Katerra · Connect · Cover · Blokable
what-went-wrong.body_html:
Every previous attempt at "factory-built housing at scale" tried to own design, factory, AND construction simultaneously. That requires hundreds of millions of dollars before the system can show whether it works.

Common failure modes

  • Vertical integration overreach — running design, factory, and construction is three companies in a trench coat
  • Capital intensity ahead of demand — built the factories before the orders
  • Custom homes in a factory — kept selling bespoke, lost the platform advantage

What FBX does differently

FBX is a software layer on top of the existing SIP/CFS manufacturer network, distributed to a franchise of trained constructors. We don't own the factories. We coordinate them.

network-effect.title: Network Effect
network-effect.subtitle: Franchisees compound
network-effect.body_html:
Each new Home-Fab makes the network more valuable to the next franchisee. Why:

  • Better supplier pricing (volume tier crosses thresholds)
  • Larger labor pool (training transfers between Fabs)
  • More R&D leverage (improvements ship to all)
  • Marketing efficiency (national brand vs. regional)

This is the moat the predecessor companies never had — they were single-factory operations.

economy-of-scale.title: Economy of Scale
economy-of-scale.subtitle: Aggregated procurement
economy-of-scale.body_html:
The 139-fab network buys panels, pods, fasteners, finishes, and equipment at volumes no individual builder can approach. Estimated 18–25% input-cost advantage at steady state vs. comparable custom builders.

Scale also disciplines the supply side: vendors who can't meet our quality bar lose the network. The aggregation IS the standard.

brand.title: Brand
brand.subtitle: Tesla / Carmax for housing
brand.body_html:
People want to trust the brand but want the uniqueness of iterative design. Automobiles cracked this fifty years ago — standardized platforms, configurable trims. HomeFab is the same idea, applied to housing.

Brand value compounds with deliveries: every finished home is an ad for the network. The franchise gets the local relationship; FBX gets the national signal.

embedding-tech.title: Embedding Technology
embedding-tech.subtitle: Run on FBX systems
embedding-tech.body_html:
Configurator, supplier ordering, dispatch, QA dossier, customer portal — every operational layer runs on FBX systems. Franchisees benefit from the toolchain; switching cost is the toolchain.

This is the "embedding" defensibility from the four-moats framework. Once a franchise is running on our stack, replicating elsewhere requires rebuilding the stack.

linear-exponential.title: Linear Cost · Exponential Output
linear-exponential.subtitle: The operating principle
linear-exponential.body_html:
Adding another Home-Fab adds one franchise's-worth of cost, but adds one franchise's-worth of output to a system that already has demand-side network effects. Output compounds; cost increments.

This is what the modular incumbents missed: they tried to scale a single factory linearly. We're scaling a network exponentially.

momentum.title: Momentum
momentum.subtitle: Speed before moats
momentum.body_html:
Moats emerge from scale, and scale comes from momentum. The first 12–24 months are about getting signed contracts and delivered homes — not about defensibility. Defensibility shows up at volume; volume comes from speed.

This is why we lead with the floor (599 contracted) and the catalyst ($4M raise) before the moats discussion. The moats are real, but they're a consequence of momentum, not a substitute for it.

adoption.title: Adoption
adoption.subtitle: Repeat behavior · contract velocity
adoption.body_html:
Adoption is measured in two dimensions:

  • Repeat behavior — franchises ordering more units after pilot
  • Contract velocity — month-over-month signed contracts

Both metrics are tracked at Core and reported quarterly. Targets escalate with regional saturation.

expansion-map.title: 10-Year Expansion Map
expansion-map.subtitle: Year 1 → Year 10 · live tool
expansion-map.body_html:
The map at slide 19 shows the year-by-year fab-rollout plan: 139 fabs across 50 states by Year 10. Each fab placement is geographically reasoned (population, regulatory climate, supply proximity).

The map is interactive — you can scrub through years and see the network grow. Numbers underneath each fab show projected annual unit output.

feasibility.title: Feasibility
feasibility.subtitle: Year-10 economics · $39.8B revenue
feasibility.body_html:
At steady-state Year-10:

  • 139 fabs × 1,090 homes/yr avg = ~149,000 homes/yr
  • $86,293 avg revenue per home — $39.8B network revenue
  • $21,607 op profit per home — $3.2B network EBITDA
  • ~25% margin at scale

The model is in the live tool at slide 13 (Year-10 Economics). Numbers update as inputs change.

truth-stamping.title: Code & Stamping
truth-stamping.subtitle: Per-jurisdiction PE partnerships
truth-stamping.body_html:
Output has to be stamped by licensed engineers, jurisdiction by jurisdiction. Not a feature flag — a real operations layer with PE partnerships in each target state.

Approach

Start in 3–5 permissive states. Expand on demand as franchise rollout reaches new regions.

Not a research problem. A solvable operations problem with a clear sequence.

truth-mfr-abstraction.title: Manufacturer Abstraction
truth-mfr-abstraction.subtitle: One design file → 2–3 quotes
truth-mfr-abstraction.body_html:
Each SIP manufacturer has its own panel specs, splines, and tolerances. One design file → 2–3 manufacturer quotes. The kit-of-parts standardization the industry never bothered to build.

Approach

Clean abstraction layer with per-manufacturer config. Suppliers compete on price and lead time without forcing the customer or the design tool to know about it.

truth-transport.title: Transport & Crane Planning
truth-transport.subtitle: Logistics-aware constraint solver
truth-transport.body_html:
A design that's perfect on paper but needs a crane the local rental yard doesn't carry is a failed design. The constraint solver has to know logistics, not just geometry.

Approach

Site-aware solver with regional equipment lookups. Transport, crane reach, and access road constraints are inputs, not afterthoughts.

truth-builder-network.title: Builder Network
truth-builder-network.subtitle: Last Mile franchise as the install layer
truth-builder-network.body_html:
Even a perfect kit needs a crew that can erect it. The Last Mile franchise is exactly this layer — vetted installers and GCs who learn the system and own the customer relationship.

Approach

Already in the deck — the franchise model is the operational answer to the "who actually builds it" question.

See Last Mile section above and the Construction Workforce card for the workforce thesis.

obj-scale.title: How big can this get? Homes per year?
obj-scale.subtitle: The scale objection
obj-scale.body_html:
Review the live Expansion Map (in this reference, section 5) and the Year-10 Economics model.

The plan is 149,490 homes delivered by Year 10 at a 43,980/yr run-rate. We can dial it more aggressive or more conservative — the model recalcs live as franchise pace, geography, and unit economics change.

obj-moat.title: What is your moat? How do you fend off copycats?
obj-moat.subtitle: The defensibility objection
obj-moat.body_html:
Wrong question at this stage.

See the moat material in Modular Industry → Why This Will Work. Moats emerge from scale, and scale comes from momentum. We're engineered for all four a16z defensibilities (network effects, scale economics, brand, embedding) once we hit volume.

First-mover speed is the strategy. The defensibility is a consequence of running fast enough to compound, not a substitute for it.

obj-national.title: Are you national, or competing with Lennar / Champion?
obj-national.subtitle: The competition objection
obj-national.body_html:
Adding to the market — demand is enormous and we're not zero-sum with the incumbents.

Our edge is speed and cost of capital, which is why the moat matters more than the head-to-head comparison. Lennar and Champion can keep doing what they do; we're building a different operating system on top of an industry the incumbents have collectively under-served for two decades.
